data.title: The focus of AI payment is not the payment itself.
data.description: "Please reorder the milk tea I bought from that shop yesterday." If one day you could actually say this to your phone, and it would find the merchant, confirm the product, place the order, pay, and arrange delivery, you probably wouldn't first think of a payment revolution. You'd likely just feel that your phone is finally starting to act as a capable assistant. This might be a more appropriate starting point for our discussion of AI payments today. Most people's understanding of AI payments isn't about giving AI a wallet, nor is it about letting a robot decide how to spend money. What people truly experience is whether a single sentence can directly become a transaction. You state your needs, and the system handles the rest. Therefore, what's truly worth discussing about AI payments isn't often the word "payment," as that word is too abstract and distant. For most internet users, the general feeling is: an app or AI assistant can handle their shopping needs directly when they express them in plain language. If we only look at the Chinese market, AI payment isn't actually that unfamiliar. The reason is simple. Chinese users already live in a very mature mobile internet ecosystem: food delivery, e-commerce, maps, ride-hailing, local services, mobile payment, and instant delivery are all interconnected. Often, what users want isn't a completely new payment method, but rather to avoid having to switch between apps and repeatedly click the same buttons. In China, AI payment is more like an upgrade to the e-commerce shopping experience than a complete reconstruction of the payment system from scratch. Alibaba's upgrade of the Qwen App in January this year is a typical example of this direction. According to the official introduction, Qwen has integrated with services within the Alibaba ecosystem, including Taobao, Taobao Flash Sale, Alipay, Fliggy, and Gaode Maps. Users can order food, complete payments in chat, and plan and book trips with just a sentence. It seems like AI has become smarter, but more accurately, it's the platform's existing capabilities that have been reorganized. This is why AI payment is most easily implemented in China not in grand visions, but in very everyday scenarios. For example, "Buy another box of the printer paper I bought last week and deliver it to my office." Or, "Order another delivery from that restaurant yesterday and have it delivered to my home." Or, "Book a restaurant close to the office, for four people, with a budget under 500 yuan." Users understand these commands immediately. Because the AI ​​here isn't some mysterious new financial tool; it's simply a smarter shopping assistant. What the people need, the endlessly competitive Chinese internet giants will do. Today, there are roughly three paths to AI-powered payments. One is to let AI watch the screen and operate on your behalf, clicking buttons, filling in information, and submitting the process like a human. The advantage of this approach is that it can be tested anywhere, but the disadvantages are also obvious: problems easily arise when the page changes, and you often still need to confirm the key parts yourself. For example, the Doubao phone, which was immediately attacked by WeChat upon its release. Another approach is for the platform to simply hand over its service capabilities directly to AI. For Chinese giants, this path is actually the smoothest. Because payment, merchants, maps, orders, and delivery are already under their control. AI doesn't need to imitate the user clicking around; it can directly access the capabilities within the system. In terms of user experience, it's "I said one thing, and it actually did it for me." For example, Alibaba's Qianwen and ByteDance's Doubao. The third approach is even more difficult, involving cross-platform, cross-website, and cross-company collaborations. At this stage, the issue isn't just about product usability, but whether others are willing to let your AI represent users in this process. Currently, we haven't seen any Chinese internet companies attempting this hard-core approach. Today, China's greatest advantage clearly lies in the first two approaches, especially the second. This is why, for Chinese users, AI payment may not seem groundbreaking; their overall feeling is likely not "AI payment is here," but rather "mobile shopping is more convenient." However, once the perspective shifts overseas, things become somewhat different. While e-commerce, food delivery, maps, and payments also exist overseas, they lack the highly integrated platform structure found in China. Service entry points, merchant systems, payment tools, and fulfillment networks are often controlled by different companies. Therefore, when AI attempts to complete a transaction on behalf of a user, the challenge isn't just "can the order be placed?", but "will others accept you as an AI agent?" This explains why AI payments overseas have become so complex. A prime example is the conflict between Amazon and Perplexity. Perplexity's Comet browser touted AI shopping as a key selling point, stating in its official description that it can help users compare products, read reviews, and proceed all the way to checkout. This aligns with many people's most intuitive imagination of AI shopping: instead of browsing websites one by one, AI will pick and buy for you. However, this is precisely where the problem lies. According to court documents dated March 9, 2026, a core argument Amazon made in its lawsuit was that Perplexity's AI agent accessed user accounts and attempted to perform actions "with the user's consent, but without Amazon's consent." Amazon's reasons for disagreeing are largely the same as WeChat's reasons for disagreeing with Doubao Mobile: authorizing AI doesn't mean the website also authorizes it. Your willingness to let it buy things for you doesn't mean the platform is willing to allow a third-party agent into its system to initiate transactions on your behalf. While the stated reason is for customer privacy and security, the core concern is that AI shopping will directly divert billions of dollars of advertising revenue from its sellers. After all, AI shopping is straightforward; it goes straight for the product and doesn't care about ads. In China, these kinds of conflicts are often resolved by the platform's integrated capabilities; however, overseas, once AI needs to complete transactions across websites, merchants, and payment networks, these issues become unavoidable. Therefore, overseas, the primary question for AI payments isn't "how easy it is to use," but rather "whether this can be completed in a closed-loop end-to-end manner." This is why Google, Stripe, and Coinbase have recently been focusing their efforts on this area. They all see the same problem: in the past, internet transactions assumed that the order was placed by a human, who was sitting in front of the screen, browsing, confirming, and paying. But once the order is placed by an authorized AI, that assumption no longer holds true. How does the website know that this AI was truly sent by them? How can the merchant confirm that it hasn't made any unauthorized purchases? How should payment institutions determine whether or not to release the funds? Google aims to first resolve the fundamental trust issue. Launching AP2 in September 2025, it wasn't about teaching AI how to make payments, but rather about answering the fundamental question: when AI initiates a payment on behalf of a user, what makes the system believe it's genuine? Google's approach is to add a verifiable "authorization certificate" to these transactions. AP2 incorporates two crucial elements: a Cart Mandate, which can be understood as the user's signature confirming the specific purchase; and a Payment Mandate, shown to the payment network and card issuer, informing them that the transaction was initiated by an agent, whether the user was present, and the transaction's context. Google later integrated this system with PayPal, creating a more complete merchant solution: merchants can use their own conversational shopping assistant to serve users, and at the payment stage, PayPal Agent takes over, using AP2 to complete the authorization and payment process. Ultimately, Google's approach addresses the fundamental question of "why trust AI to place orders." Stripe's approach is closer to merchants and platforms. It actually involves two layers. The first layer is the ACP, or Agentic Commerce Protocol, launched in September 2025 with OpenAI. You can think of it as a "standard for AI-readable checkout." Stripe doesn't want AI to mimic human actions like clicking web pages and filling out forms; instead, it wants merchants to proactively open up their capabilities regarding products, inventory, and checkout, allowing AI to initiate purchases using a standardized interface. The advantage of this approach is that merchants remain primarily responsible for orders and fulfillment; product display, order processing, and risk control assessments are still under their control, but they don't need to rebuild their systems for each AI platform individually. Stripe later launched the Agentic Commerce Suite, making catalog access, checkout, and payment a one-stop tool to lower the barrier to entry for merchants. However, Stripe also saw a completely different scenario. It's not "AI helping people buy things," but rather "software paying for software directly." Therefore, in March 2026, it launched Machine Payments. In this scenario, AI doesn't buy a Coke, but rather APIs, data, computing power, content access rights, or even pay-per-use services. Stripe's solution is that merchants can directly convert their interfaces to pay-per-use rates, as low as 0.01 USDC, with the money going into Stripe's account, and final settlement and reconciliation still done in Stripe's familiar way. For agents, there's no need to register an account, apply for an API key, or go through a bunch of manual processes; they can make payments while making inquiries. In other words, Stripe is building both an AI shopping checkout system and a micro-payment track between machines. Coinbase takes a different approach. It's more like assuming that in the future, many AIs won't be "shopping assistants" but rather "software with wallets." Therefore, in February 2026, it launched Agentic Wallets. The core isn't to make AI better at browsing websites, but to give AI a real wallet that can spend, receive, and transact, and this wallet has security boundaries. Coinbase's system includes the wallet infrastructure itself, the x402 machine payment protocol, and pre-packaged capabilities such as deposits, transfers, transactions, and earnings management. Its main focus is on solving a different type of problem: if an AI wants to buy computing power, data, pay API fees, or complete some kind of automated transaction on-chain, can it avoid waiting for confirmation every time? Coinbase's official documentation calls this agent an "independent economic agent." This term is a bit strong, but the meaning is clear: what Coinbase wants to do is not to make AI more like a shopping assistant, but to make AI itself a software entity that can handle income, expenditure, settlement, and transactions. If you look at these three paths together, the differences become clear. Google wants to first solve "how do you prove that this transaction was truly authorized by me?" Stripe is more concerned with "how merchants can connect their products and checkout capabilities to AI." Coinbase, on the other hand, has taken a step further to solve "if the payer is software, how does it complete income and expenditure on its own?" This is precisely why, while they all appear to be working on AI payments, their focuses differ. Google is more like adding a layer of trust to cross-platform intermediary transactions; Stripe aims to integrate AI shopping and AI-driven service calls into existing business networks; Coinbase, on the other hand, is betting on a world where software directly pays for software. At this point, the difference becomes clear. In China, AI payments are more like a natural extension of a mature internet system. Platforms have already organized merchants, payments, delivery, maps, and local services; AI's role is to rearrange these capabilities, allowing users to go from "operating themselves" to "simply speaking." Overseas, AI payments are more like a belated catch-up. Because no platform naturally controls the entire transaction chain, once AI is to actually complete transactions for users, it immediately encounters real-world problems such as whether websites will approve it, whether merchants will accept it, whether payment institutions will recognize it, and who will be responsible if problems arise. In China, it's more like pushing the online shopping experience a step further. Overseas, it's more like renegotiating the rules for participation by multiple parties in internet software and online consumption scenarios. This is why Chinese users' first reaction to AI payments might be, "This function is quite convenient"; while overseas markets' perception of AI payments is more like, "The original online transaction methods may need to be changed." Returning to the initial scenario... "Please place another order for the milk tea I bought yesterday and deliver it to my door." The truly interesting part of this sentence isn't the word "payment," but rather how it delegates many tasks that you would normally have to do manually to the system. It needs to know which store you mentioned, which item, its specifications, delivery location, and payment method. Payment is important, of course, but it's just the least significant step in the whole process. Therefore, the focus of AI payment isn't the payment itself. What people really care about isn't which protocol the backend uses, whether it's on-chain settlement, whether it uses stablecoins, or whether it uses encryption algorithms. Ultimately, people will use a very simple standard to judge its usefulness: I told the AI ​​something, can it actually get it done for me?

data.title: Tokens become "money-devouring beasts," and the computing power of major companies has skyrocketed.
data.description:
Have you noticed how quickly the AI ​​industry has changed in the last six months? This time last year, everyone was celebrating the price reduction of computing power. Alibaba Cloud led the charge, announcing "up to 60% price cuts," and Tencent Cloud, Huawei Cloud, and Baidu Cloud all followed suit—it was like a Double Eleven shopping festival. In startup groups, people were constantly showing off their bills: "Look, I only spent a few cents on this million in tokens!" Back then, everyone thought the spring of AI startups had arrived; computing power was as cheap as cabbage, who couldn't afford large-scale model applications? And what happened? The reality check came incredibly quickly. Just last month, the winds completely reversed. Google, Amazon, Tencent, Alibaba, and Baidu all issued price increase announcements within 10 days. How much? Generally, 30% to 50%. The most drastic increase was by Tencent Cloud, with one core product seeing a direct price hike of 400%. From "fire sale" to "rocket-like price hikes," less than a year has passed. What exactly happened? Who is behind the scenes pushing prices up? More importantly, in this wave of price increases, who suffered the most, and who is secretly laughing? Last year they were offering "huge discounts," so why the collective price increases this year? Let's briefly review this dramatic reversal. In April 2025, Alibaba Cloud dropped a bombshell: core products saw price reductions of up to 60%. This wasn't a small-scale operation; it was a genuine "half-price cut followed by a discount." JD Cloud immediately followed suit, saying, "Lower your prices as much as you want, I'll follow," and Tencent Cloud, Huawei Cloud, and Baidu Cloud all followed suit. Suddenly, the computing power market was ablaze with competition, and the price war was fierce. What were the slogans back then? "Make AI affordable" and "Make computing power accessible to all." Many startups actually believed them and started burning through tokens and running models on a large scale. However, a free lunch never lasts long. In January 2026, Amazon AWS quietly did something—without any press conference or prior notice, it directly raised the price of its EC2 servers by approximately 15%. Don't underestimate the seemingly small increase; it's significant: this is the first price hike in the cloud services industry in nearly two decades. Keep in mind that in the past two decades, AWS has lowered prices over a hundred times, always going down, never up. This is like toppling a domino effect.

3Month11On Japan, Tencent Cloud followed up, and its Tencent HY2.0 Instructmodel input price started from 0.0008Yuan/ThousandstokensRising to0.004505yuan/Thousandstokens - an increase of 463%, more than four times. On March 18th, Alibaba Cloud announced a price increase for its computing power card products, from 5% to 34%. Baidu AI Cloud followed suit, increasing its price from 5% to 30%. Those large models that were previously available for free in public beta, such as GLM 5, MiniMax 2.5, and Kimi 2.5, have all ended their "free trial" period and moved to official billing. From "rushing to lower prices" to "rushing to raise prices," why has the change been so rapid? On the surface, it seems cloud vendors are struggling to cope. GPU chips are becoming increasingly expensive, and electricity costs for data centers account for 40% to 60% of operating costs. Furthermore, memory chip prices also began to rise in the second half of 2025, putting real pressure on costs. But what truly made the price increase "inevitable" was another, more fundamental reason—computing power was genuinely insufficient. Who's frantically "eating" tokens? The truth behind 140 trillion. Wasn't there supposed to be a surplus of computing power before? How come it's suddenly insufficient? The answer is: Token was "eaten" too quickly. According to data disclosed by Liu Liehong, Director of the National Bureau of Data Science and Technology, in March of this year: By March 2026, the daily number of token calls in China had exceeded 140. trillions. How staggering is this number? Here are two points for reference: At the beginning of 2024, this number was only 100 billion. In two years, it increased more than a thousandfold.

· 2025At the end of the year, the number is100Trillion. In other words, in just three months, it has increased by another 40%—the increase in just these three months (40 trillion yuan) is 400 times that of the entire day at the beginning of 2024. This is not linear growth; it's a tsunami. So the question is: Who is consuming tokens like crazy? The answer is one word: Agent. Starting last year, products represented by the open-source AI agent OpenClaw (affectionately known as "Lobster") have become incredibly popular. AI has evolved from a robot that can only "chat" into a helper that can "get things done"—booking flights, writing code, creating PPTs, analyzing data… Sounds pretty cool, right? However, the cost is that for an intelligent agent to perform a simple task, the amount of tokens consumed is 10 to 100 times that of a normal conversation. For example, having an AI write a web crawler script for you. In a normal conversation, it gives you a piece of code, you copy it and leave, consuming a few hundred tokens. But if it's an intelligent agent, it has to run the code itself, report errors, debug, run it again, debug again... going back and forth dozens of times, consuming tens of thousands of tokens. Not to mention video generation, which is a "money-devouring beast." Analysis indicates that generating a 1-minute video would consume approximately 10 trillion tokens. However, current video models charge only a few cents to a few dollars for generating a 5-second video—this isn't making money; it's clearly losing money to gain publicity. But the sheer number of users is overwhelming; videos, music, code, data analysis… every field is frantically “consuming” tokens. Supply can't keep up with demand, so computing power naturally goes up in price. This isn't a conspiracy; it's a blatant supply-demand imbalance. Computing Power Ranking Competition: Big Companies Eat the Meat, Small Players Can't Even Get a Sip. The price increase means completely different things to different people. For cloud vendors, price increases are actually a good thing. A brokerage firm has calculated that for every 1% price increase by Alibaba Cloud, its profit margin increases by 1 percentage point. Therefore, the data you see shows that Alibaba Cloud's market share has increased rather than decreased, now accounting for 36% of China's AI cloud market. In the more specific segment of AI usage, Volcano Engine (owned by ByteDance) accounts for nearly 50%—meaning that half of all token usage in China goes through Volcano Engine's pipeline. Meanwhile, the market share of Huawei Cloud and Tencent Cloud is slightly declining. The head effect is becoming increasingly apparent: large companies are getting stronger, and resources are becoming more concentrated. Who's suffering the most? Small and medium-sized AI startups, and those newcomers to the field. The reason is simple: the price increase directly drives up their operating costs. Previously, when tokens were cheap, you could run experiments and adjust models freely without spending much money. Now, with prices several times or even ten times higher, every round of training and every inference requires careful consideration. What's more troublesome is that smaller players have no bargaining power. Large clients can sign long-term agreements with cloud providers, locking in a relatively favorable price. As a startup, spending only tens of thousands of yuan a year on computing power, who's going to negotiate a discount with you? You can only obediently pay the increased price. Many projects that originally planned to develop AI applications quietly shelved them after doing the math. Others that were already in progress either scaled back or stubbornly borne the losses. And the terminal market is fiercely competitive; you can't easily raise prices for users—the competitors are still offering it for free, and if you start charging, all your users will leave. In the end, you have to swallow all the cost pressure yourself. An industry insider complained to me: "I used to think that cheap computing power meant a lower barrier to entry for startups. Now I realize that the barrier isn't lower; it's that they let you in first, then shut the door on you." This is actually a brutal ranking competition. For the past twenty years, cloud vendors have thrived on a strategy of "low prices for volume, securing market share first, then profiting." But that era is over. Computing power has officially bid farewell to the subsidy period and entered the commercial pricing stage. The future competition will no longer be about who is cheaper, but about whose service is more stable, whose ecosystem is more complete, and who can truly help enterprises make the best use of every bit of computing power. In this ranking competition, smaller players are likely to be left behind. Looking back at the rollercoaster ride of the past year, you'll find a rather sobering truth: the shift in computing power from "dirt cheap" to "rocket-priced" is essentially a microcosm of the AI ​​industry's transition from unregulated growth to maturity. The era of free services is over; value-based competition has begun. Business models that rely on subsidies will die out, while products with genuine technology, real-world applications, and a user base will survive, and even thrive, in an environment of rising computing power costs. The core competitiveness of AI startups has never been about how cheap computing power is, but rather what you do with that computing power. In the computing power era, tokens are indeed expensive. But more valuable than a token is a mind that knows how to use tokens effectively.

data.title: Trump's son has been exposed for profiting from the war, and his family is suspected of war-related corruption.
data.description:
Powerus, a drone company partially owned by Donald Trump Jr. and Eric Trump, Trump's two sons, is selling drone interceptors to Gulf states that are facing attacks from Iran and rely on the protection of the U.S. military.

According to an Associated Press report on April 3, Powerus co-founder Brett Velicovich told the Associated Press that the company is marketing its drone interceptors to some Gulf states, conducting drone interception demonstrations to show how Powerus' defensive drone interceptors can help these countries defend against Iranian attacks.

"Our team is currently demonstrating the interceptor across the Middle East," said Veljkovic. "We have very advanced technology that can save lives." He did not disclose further details regarding which countries Powerus is marketing to. Former White House ethics lawyer Richard Payne, speaking under the George W. Bush administration, commented that the Trump family will become the first presidential family in history to profit excessively from war. "These (Gulf) countries are under immense pressure to buy weapons from the president's sons so that the president will act according to their wishes." Powerus's expansion is inseparable from the support of Donald Trump Jr. and Eric Trump, both of whom are significant shareholders in Powerus. Neither Donald Trump Jr. nor Eric Trump have responded to reports of Powerus marketing drone interceptors to the Middle East. Since Trump's return to the White House, Donald Trump Jr. and Eric Trump have aggressively expanded the Trump family's business empire, with ventures including cryptocurrency, military industry, rocket components, market forecasting, and rare earth magnets. According to an Associated Press report on March 12, Powerus previously won a government drone tender worth up to $1.1 billion. By becoming shareholders in Powerus, Trump's two sons could potentially reap substantial profits. When asked about potential nepotism in the bidding process, Eric Trump stated, "I'm very proud to be able to invest in companies I approve of. Drones are the future." Veljkovic added that the Trump sons' investment was unrelated to the company's winning bid, stating, "Our company's focus is not on politics." Powerus was founded in 2025. According to a previous Reuters report, on March 9th, with the support of Trump's two sons, Powerus merged with Aureus Greenway Holdings, a publicly traded company primarily operating golf courses, successfully achieving a backdoor listing. Aureus Greenway Holdings also has close ties to Trump's two sons.

data.title: The Next Generation Internet – AI, Blockchain, and the Machine Economy
data.description:
When AI no longer merely answers questions but begins to act on behalf of humans, a fundamental question arises: How can machines pay for, authorize, and leave a credible record of their actions in the real world? Over the past two years, discussions about the combination of AI and blockchain have finally shifted from the narrative of "leveraging" to the pragmatic construction of "infrastructure coupling." As outlined by a16z last year, the intersection of the two is forming a new internet architecture around identity, data, payment, ownership, and collaboration. By 2026, the market's focus will have completely shifted from "what generative AI can do" to "how AI agents participate in real economic activities." This means that the role of blockchain is undergoing a silent but profound transformation: it is evolving from a synonym for crypto assets and speculation into a settlement layer, identity layer, authorization layer, and audit layer in the era of AI agents. Only when AI truly begins to "act" will blockchain transform from a secondary technology into a necessity-based infrastructure. Three major new changes: a16z's views last year were like a precise "map," while the industry development over the past year has been like building roads on that map. The most significant changes are threefold: 1. AI Agents Become the Main Narrative By 2026, AI agents have transformed from a concept into a core industry element. For example, Base, in its 2026 roadmap, explicitly prioritizes the "AI agent economy," aiming to enable agents to perform actions with money through smart accounts, payment protocols, and more. This signifies that the industry has entered a new phase where AI acts on behalf of others: once an agent possesses executive power, it requires wallets, permissions, and identity—precisely where the inherent advantages of blockchain and stablecoins lie. AI agents may become the core driver of the next wave of on-chain interaction growth. 2. "Verifiability" Becomes a Central Theme With the proliferation of AI-generated content, market demand for traceability of source, authenticity, and responsibility has surged. Institutions are facing pressure to prove data origins, model decisions, and attribution of responsibility. Blockchain's immutable records, timestamps, and audit trails are being seen as key underlying capabilities to meet these "decision traceability" requirements. Future competition will not only be about computing power, but also about the ability to create trustworthy records. 3. Identity and Permissions Take Precedence Over Computing Power Unlike the earlier hot topic of "decentralized computing power," this year's more realistic focus is on identity, permissions, reputation, and authorization. a16z emphasized in its discussions earlier this year that blockchain can support persistent user context for AI agents, portable identities, and on-chain payments without intermediaries. The most likely products to mature in the short term are those that allow AI agents to have restricted wallets, allow users to authorize AI to use specific data, and allow enterprises to audit the complete action chain of AI. Based on the above changes, the integration of AI and blockchain is focusing on four distinct intersections: AI-assisted payments: This is currently the direction with the greatest commercial potential. Stablecoins and on-chain payments are naturally suited for small-amount, high-frequency, automated machine-to-machine (M2M) transactions. AI agents can automatically purchase APIs, subscribe to services, or execute payments. This is transforming the crypto economy from an "asset system" into a "payment layer for the machine economy." Verifiable AI: The goal is not to make AI smarter, but to make it more trustworthy. It focuses on model version, input data source, whether the output has been tampered with, and whether the execution process can be audited. The value of blockchain lies in providing traceable and verifiable records for critical decision-making events, which is crucial for high-risk areas such as finance, healthcare, and compliance. User-Controlled Data and Context: The more intelligent AI becomes, the more it needs continuous user context. Blockchain and related protocols allow users to carry their authorizations, credentials, and historical records between different services, achieving data portability and sovereignty. This will give rise to new data business models: users can authorize, price, and audit their own data and behavior. Trustworthy Content and Traceability: In an era of AI-generated content overload, the authenticity of information is difficult to verify. Blockchain can serve as a "proof layer," recording the creation, editing, and signing history of content, providing a foundation for verifying the authenticity of media, copyright, and even corporate documents, and combating the risk of systemic information pollution. The historic convergence of two technology curves is a critical window of opportunity because AI and blockchain are simultaneously entering a phase of infrastructure restructuring: AI is evolving from a tool to an agent, while crypto is shifting from a speculative asset to a payment, identity, and settlement infrastructure. The convergence of these two technology curves in 2026 will give rise to a new narrative that "machines also need economic identity." In the next 1-3 years, AI & Blockchain may advance along the following timeline: Short term (1-2 years): Payment and identity will mature first. This is the direction closest to commercial value and with the least technological friction; enterprises will prioritize solving API payments, subscription settlements, and permission verification for agents. Mid-term (2-3 years): Verifiable workflows will become standard capabilities. The complete AI workflow—from data input and model invocation to output—will universally require on-chain audit traces. Long-term (3+ years): A true machine economy/agent economy may emerge. AI agents will be able to autonomously trade services, purchase data, and collaborate to complete tasks, while blockchain will become the universal language for negotiation, settlement, and trust building between machines. The opportunities within this are clearly visible: stablecoin-driven AI agent payments, on-chain identity and authorization, verifiable AI services for high-value markets, and content traceability to combat information pollution all hold enormous potential. However, risks should not be ignored: Overly enthusiastic narratives coupled with insufficient practical application may create a bubble; the allocation of responsibility for errors made by AI agents remains a legal blind spot; user experience barriers for wallets, keys, etc., remain high; furthermore, centralized AI platforms may internalize "trusted agent payment" as a platform function, thereby squeezing the development space for open protocols.

Conclusion

If we consider a16z's article from last year as a "map of AI x blockchain," then the progress in 2026 shows that the real battleground is no longer "whether AI is on the blockchain," but rather who can provide the entire infrastructure stack of identity, payment, authorization, auditing, and trusted records once AI agents enter the real economy. In short: The ultimate significance of blockchain for AI is not to turn AI into on-chain applications, but to enable AI to safely enter and transform the real world in a trustworthy, controllable, and verifiable manner. The dawn of the machine economy may have already quietly begun with lines of smart contract code and countless automated payments.

data.title: Cracking Bitcoin in 9 Minutes? The Boundaries and Misinterpretations of Google's Quantum White Paper
data.description:
By: Max He @ Safeheron Lab

Core Judgment of this Article

  • Google's white paper significantly advances the engineering assessment of quantum risks, but does not prove that CRQC is close to practical implementation

  • A decrease in resource estimation ≠ the availability of real-world attack capabilities; many engineering challenges remain to be overcome

  • The industry needs to build not only the ability to "adopt post-quantum algorithms," but also the ability to "cope with the continuous changes in cryptography."

  • 2030–2035 is the key reference window for backward migration preparation, not the precise time point of quantum attack arrival

On March 30, 2026, Google Quantum AI, together with researchers from the Ethereum Foundation and Stanford University, released a major white paper [1]. This 57-page paper systematically analyzes the threat posed by quantum computing to cryptocurrencies and provides the most aggressive resource estimate to date: Breaking the 256-bit elliptic curve cryptography upon which Bitcoin and Ethereum rely requires fewer than 500,000 physical qubits—nearly 20 times less than the previous best estimate. Simultaneously, the paper expands the discussion of quantum attacks from Bitcoin to the entire cryptocurrency ecosystem, further pointing out that Ethereum's smart contracts, staking consensus, and data availability sampling mechanisms also possess potential quantum attack surfaces. This means that this white paper is no longer just discussing the single issue of whether Bitcoin private keys can be quantum-cracked, but is prompting the entire industry to re-examine which security assumptions of existing blockchain systems may need to be re-evaluated in the face of the evolution of quantum capabilities. This white paper has caused a significant stir in the blockchain industry. The claim that "quantum computing could crack Bitcoin in minutes" spread rapidly, prompting many practitioners to re-examine existing security assumptions. This strong reaction wasn't just due to further reductions in resource estimates, but also because it placed the "possibility of on-chain transaction window attacks" and the "whether blockchain systems can complete the migration in time" on the same level of discussion for the first time. The question shifted from the academic question of "can it be cracked?" to the engineering and governance question of "is there enough time to prepare?" However, behind these sentiments, a more worthy question to ask is: What has Google actually proven? And what hasn't it proven? To what extent has this work changed our understanding of quantum risks? It's worth noting that the white paper's impact extends beyond Bitcoin-style key exposure issues to the broader attack surface of cryptocurrency systems. However, this article focuses more on the changes this work brings to the overall assessment of quantum risks, rather than detailing the specific impacts of different on-chain mechanisms.

1 What exactly did Google do this time?

1.1 ECDLP: The basic assumption of blockchain security

The security of current mainstream cryptocurrencies is based on the Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP) [2]. Taking the secp256k1 curve used by Bitcoin and Ethereum as an example [3], its core assumption is that under classical computing conditions, given a public key (a point on the elliptic curve), it is impossible to derive the corresponding private key in feasible time. This assumption has been widely accepted over the past few decades and constitutes the basic security premise of the entire blockchain system. However, Shor's algorithm [4] points out that under an ideal quantum computing model, ECDLP can be solved efficiently, thus theoretically shaking this security foundation. 1.2 Resource Estimation: How Much Quantum Computing Power Would Be Required to Crack ECDLP? The core of Google's work this time is not to propose a new attack method, but to re-answer a long-standing question: If a sufficiently large and stable quantum computer capable of running such quantum algorithms can be built in the future, how much computational resources would be needed to crack ECDLP? The paper constructs and optimizes quantum circuits for secp256k1 and provides two different optimization paths: one to minimize the number of logical qubits, and the other to minimize the number of non-Clifford gates (such as Toffoli gates). Under a set of explicit hardware and error correction assumptions, these circuits can be executed on a scale of fewer than 500,000 physical qubits. Compared with previous mainstream estimates, [5][6], this result shows a significant improvement in the comprehensive indicator of "spacetime volume". More importantly, it transforms the originally theoretical discussion into a set of comparable and traceable engineering parameters. 1.3 "9 minutes": How was this number derived? In addition to resource estimation, the paper also provides an intuitive order of magnitude for attack time. Assuming quantum gate operation times are on the order of microseconds and considering certain execution overhead, a complete run of the relevant quantum circuit would take approximately ten minutes. Considering that some computations in the quantum algorithm can be pre-completed before the public key is available, the computations actually related to the target public key can be compressed to about half the time, resulting in an estimate of "approximately 9 minutes." This figure has attracted widespread attention because it is close to Bitcoin's average block time of approximately 10 minutes. This means that, under certain assumptions, an attacker could theoretically recover the private key before transaction confirmation. It is important to emphasize that this time estimate relies on a set of idealized premises and its significance lies more in providing a magnitude reference than a direct reflection of actual attack capabilities.

1.4 Zero-knowledge proof: Why not disclose the circuit

Another important feature of the paper is that it introduces a "verifiable disclosure" method without disclosing the specific quantum circuit.[7].

The research team committed to the circuit through hashing and checked the circuit's behavior on a set of random inputs in a public verification procedure, while verifying its resource upper bound. The entire verification process was encapsulated as a zero-knowledge proof, allowing any third party to confirm the correctness of the relevant statements without touching the circuit details. This approach strikes a balance between "protecting attack details" and "improving the credibility of conclusions," making resource estimation no longer just a researcher's statement, but cryptographically verifiable. How should this be understood? Before further understanding these results, one concept deserves clarification. The paper repeatedly mentions CRQC (Cryptographically Relevant Quantum Computer). The term literally translates to "cryptography-related quantum computer," but it's not a general term for quantum computers. It refers specifically to quantum computing systems that already possess real-world cryptanalysis capabilities. In other words, what the blockchain industry should truly focus on is not whether quantum computing continues to advance, but when it will develop to the point where it can crack cryptographic problems like ECDLP under real-world conditions. From this perspective, the significance of Google's work lies not only in showcasing the progress of quantum computing itself, but also in answering a more specific question: What kind of resource scale, execution capability, and time characteristics does a quantum computer need to pose a threat to real-world cryptographic systems? Furthermore, this question can be understood from three dimensions: the execution characteristics of quantum computing systems, the different paths that technological evolution may lead to, and the attack methods that these capabilities ultimately correspond to. 2.1 Fast and Slow Clocks: There Is Not Just One Type of Quantum Computer An important perspective proposed in the paper is to distinguish between different types of quantum computing architectures. Some platforms (such as superconducting qubits) have faster basic operation speeds and shorter error correction cycles, and can execute deep circuits in a shorter time; while other platforms (such as ion traps or neutral atoms) have slower operation speeds, but may have advantages in other aspects. This difference means that "quantum computing power" is not a single indicator. Quantum systems of the same scale, under different architectures, can have actual attack capabilities against cryptographic problems that differ by orders of magnitude. This difference in execution characteristics directly affects the way and time structure of CRQC formation: some systems are closer to completing computation within a short time window, while others are more suitable for long-term operation. 2.2 Two Possible Evolution Paths Based on the above architectural differences, we can further consider the evolution paths of quantum computing power. One scenario is that quantum systems with faster execution capabilities reach fault tolerance levels first. In this case, real-time attacks on on-chain transactions (such as recovering private keys before transaction confirmation) will become the main risk. Another scenario is that slower but more stable systems achieve a breakthrough first. In this case, attacks are more likely to focus on long-exposed public keys, such as historical addresses or reused keys. These two paths are not mutually exclusive, but their corresponding risk time structures and defense priorities differ significantly. From this perspective, the emergence of CRQC does not necessarily correspond to a specific point in time, but is more likely a gradual process of acquiring different capabilities. 2.3 Three Attack Methods Within the above framework, quantum attacks can be broadly categorized into three types. The first type is the "on-spend attack," which involves recovering the private key within the time window after a transaction enters the mempool but before it is written into a block. The second type is the "at-rest attack," which targets public keys that have been exposed on the chain for a long time, giving attackers more computation time. The third type is the "on-setup attack," which targets protocols that rely on public parameters, using one-time quantum computation to obtain a reusable backdoor. These three types of attacks share the commonality of relying on the same fundamental capability—solving ECDLP within an acceptable timeframe—but their dependencies on time windows and system architecture differ. From the results, these three types of attacks are simply different manifestations of the same thing: the specific impact on different system conditions and time constraints after quantum computing power reaches the level represented by CRQC. 3 How far are we from a true quantum attack? 3.1 What This White Paper Doesn't Prove It needs to be emphasized that while this white paper significantly advances the engineering assessment of quantum risks, it doesn't prove that CRQC is close to practical implementation, nor does it prove that existing blockchain systems will face real and feasible quantum attacks in the short term. What the paper truly does is further compress the resource estimation required to break secp256k1 under a set of explicit assumptions, and move the originally abstract risk discussion to a position more suitable for engineering assessment. It proves that the relevant issues are more concrete than previously understood and more worthy of continuous monitoring; however, it doesn't prove that the large-scale fault-tolerant quantum systems needed to support these attacks are within reach. 3.2 Resource Requirements Are Decreasing, But the Engineering Gap Remains Significant Furthermore, the transition from "quantum algorithms can theoretically break ECDLP" to "the emergence of quantum computing capabilities in the real world sufficient to threaten cryptographic systems" is not simply a matter of scaling up engineering. What truly determines whether a quantum attack can be implemented is not just the estimated resource figures on paper, but also fault-tolerant architecture, error correction, real-time decoding, control systems, and the overall system capabilities required for stable execution of deep circuits over long periods. Some of these conditions are indeed engineering implementation problems; however, they cannot be simply understood as "being solved naturally sooner or later with continued investment." Quantum error correction and fault-tolerant computing theoretically offer a scalable path, but whether these conditions can be truly integrated into a sustainably operating CRQC capable of threatening real-world cryptographic systems remains significantly uncertain. From this perspective, the more accurate significance of Google's white paper is not to declare that quantum attacks are imminent, but rather to allow the industry to discuss this risk for the first time with more specific engineering parameters, while also reminding us that a decrease in resource estimates should not be directly equated with the availability of real-world attack capabilities. 3.3 This is not a problem suitable for precise year prediction. Therefore, the arrival of quantum attacks cannot be understood as a precisely predictable point in time. For the blockchain industry, what truly matters is not "which year CRQC will definitely appear," but whether related capabilities are evolving in an increasingly alarming direction. On the one hand, key breakthroughs can significantly alter resource demands in a short period; on the other hand, seemingly similar technological paths may remain stuck before certain fundamental bottlenecks for a long time. This means that it's difficult to predict when real-world attack capabilities will emerge through linear extrapolations like "how many qubits this year, how many next year." Therefore, a more prudent understanding of this issue is not to try to bet on a specific year, but to acknowledge its inherent uncertainty and focus on the underlying signals that truly change risk assessments. 3.4 The most alarming aspect is that warning signs may not be obvious. This also means that the community should not expect to obtain clear warning signs through a single "public demonstration of a quantum attack." Many people are accustomed to viewing public demonstrations as a sign of technological maturity, as if the absence of a real-world demonstration means that a real threat is still far off. However, this intuition may not hold true regarding quantum cryptography. By the time certain landmark demonstrations actually appear, the relevant capabilities may have already been accumulated at a much longer technological level, and the defense window may have already narrowed significantly. For the blockchain industry, this is precisely its most difficult aspect: Truly significant changes may not unfold in a clear, gradual, and publicly visible way. 4. How should we judge quantum progress? 4.1 Don't just look at the number of qubits. If Chapter 3 answered the question of "where we are roughly now," then the next question is: what should we look at in the future to more accurately judge quantum progress? The most easily disseminated and misunderstood indicator is the number of qubits. It's intuitive and eye-catching, but for cryptanalysis capabilities, it's far from the only, or even the most crucial, indicator. Simply increasing the number of physical qubits doesn't automatically mean the system is close to real-world attack capabilities. What's truly more important is whether these qubits can be effectively organized under fault-tolerant conditions, whether they can stably support deep circuit execution, and whether they form a closed loop with the algorithm and control system. For the industry, "how many qubits" at most only indicates a change in scale, not the proximity of a real-world threat. 4.2 Three Types of Signals That Really Deserve Attention To form a relatively workable framework for judging quantum progress, we can focus on three types of signals. The first type is hardware signals. What's truly important here is not just the number of physical qubits, but whether stable logical qubits have begun to appear, whether error correction has entered a scalable stage, and whether the system can continue to operate under error correction conditions. The second type is algorithmic signals. Google's white paper itself is a typical example. For the blockchain industry, what's more noteworthy is not a single number itself, but whether these resource estimates are continuously decreasing: whether the number of logical qubits is decreasing, whether the number of key gate operations is decreasing, and whether the overall spacetime volume continues to converge. The third category is system signals. These are often the most easily overlooked. Even with advancements in hardware and algorithms, it's still necessary to observe whether system-level capabilities are maturing, such as the ability to stably execute deep circuits over extended periods, the scalability of control systems, and whether multiple key conditions are beginning to be met simultaneously. Ultimately, real-world attack capabilities do not depend on a single indicator, but on whether these conditions can converge into a closed engineering path. 4.3 Public demonstrations can be used as a reference, but not as the sole signal. Many people naturally expect a certain "landmark moment": for example, an experimental platform publicly demonstrates running related algorithms on a small-scale curve, and everyone sees it as a signal that risks are truly beginning to emerge. Such signals certainly have reference value, but they are not suitable as the sole basis for judgment. Because from the perspective of technological evolution, public demonstrations are often just a result, not the earliest change itself. What is truly more important is whether the underlying conditions mentioned earlier have been gradually met. For the industry, a more realistic approach is not to wait for a dramatic moment, but to establish a habit of continuous tracking: observing whether hardware has entered a new stage, whether algorithm resources continue to be compressed, and whether system capabilities are moving from "distributed improvements" to "overall formation." Rather than asking "when will we see a demonstration," it's more important to ask: Before seeing a demonstration, have we already understood the direction of technological progress? 5. How should we judge quantum progress? 5.1 This is not a "present problem," but preparations must begin now. From an engineering perspective, quantum computing does not yet possess the capability to attack existing cryptocurrency systems. Whether in terms of hardware scale, error control, or the ability to stably execute deep circuits over long periods, there is still a significant gap compared to the conditions assumed in the paper. However, this does not mean the industry can continue to postpone this issue indefinitely. A significant change compared to the past is that the relevant technological paths have become increasingly clear, and resource estimations are continuously converging. For blockchain systems, the real focus should not be on a specific point in time, but rather on whether sufficient time and space have been reserved for future migrations. Upgrading cryptographic infrastructure is rarely a simple software replacement. It involves changes in protocols, implementations, ecosystem collaboration, asset migration, and user habits, typically measured in years rather than months or quarters. From this perspective, it's not a problem that "will erupt now," but one that must be planned for as early as possible. 5.2 Algorithms Will Change, But Blockchain System Design Doesn't Need to Be Overturned. Quantum computing directly impacts the cryptographic assumptions underlying blockchain systems, such as elliptic curve signature schemes, rather than the problems blockchain systems face as a type of security system. This means that many security mechanisms proven effective today will not lose their value due to the advent of quantum computing. For the blockchain and digital asset industry, whether it's key management, multi-party computation (MPC), hardware isolation (TEE), access control, auditing mechanisms, or the overall security architecture built around account systems, transaction approvals, risk control, and governance, the issues being addressed are still real-world problems such as key exposure, single points of failure, internal risks, and operational errors. These problems will not disappear with changes in the underlying cryptographic primitives. Therefore, a more reasonable understanding is not that "the quantum era requires a complete overhaul of the blockchain security system," but rather: What needs upgrading is primarily the underlying cryptographic components; what needs to be preserved and enhanced are the design principles already established in blockchain systems regarding key protection, hierarchical access control, risk isolation, and governance control. What's truly important is not just replacing a particular signature algorithm, but enabling the entire system to support such cryptographic migrations. 5.3 From “Which Algorithm to Choose” to “Smooth Transfer” Currently, post-quantum cryptography has entered the standardization and engineering implementation stage. The first batch of PQC standards led by NIST was officially released in 2024 [12], but different schemes still have significant differences in performance, signature size, implementation complexity and security assumptions, and engineering practices and industry adoption paths are still evolving. In this context, rather than betting on a specific algorithm too early, a more important question is changing: whether the system has the ability to transfer smoothly. This capability encompasses several levels: the ability to introduce new signature schemes without impacting business continuity; the ability to support hybrid modes for a period of time; and the ability to continue adjusting and remaining compatible as standards and engineering practices evolve. In the long run, what the blockchain industry truly needs to build is not just the ability to "adopt post-quantum algorithms," but the ability to "cope with continuous changes in cryptography." The former is a migration, while the latter represents a long-term sustainable system design. 6 Conclusion: An Important Technological Signal From today's engineering reality, quantum computing is still insufficient to pose a real threat to the existing cryptocurrency system. Whether it's hardware scale, error control, or the fault tolerance required for stable execution of deep circuits over long periods, there's still a significant gap compared to the conditions assumed in the paper. In other words, CRQC is not a technology that "will naturally be implemented when the time comes"; its realization still depends on a series of engineering challenges that have not yet been fully overcome. However, at the same time, this issue is no longer suitable for abstract discussions considered as a distant future. Google explicitly set its post-quantum migration timeline to 2029 in March 2026 [8]; the UK NCSC gave three key migration nodes: 2028, 2031, and 2035 [9]; the G7 Cyber ​​Expert Group's roadmap for the financial system, while not setting a regulatory deadline, also considers 2035 as a reference target for the overall migration and suggests that key systems should be migrated by 2035 as much as possible. The migration should be prioritized between 2030 and 2032. [10] At the same time, it is also necessary to avoid over-interpretation. According to the current mainstream public information, even the more aggressive public judgments are more about moving the risk window forward to around 2030, rather than forming a consensus that "CRQC will be clearly implemented before 2030". A 2025 expert survey by the Global Risk Institute showed that the occurrence of CRQC within the next 10 years is "quite possible (28%–49%)", and it will only enter the "likely (51%–70%)" range within the next 15 years. [11] Therefore, the most important significance of Google's white paper is not that it announces that quantum attacks have arrived, but that it makes this issue concrete enough for the first time: it can be discussed, evaluated, and preparations must begin. For the blockchain and digital asset industry, 2030–2035 is a crucial window that deserves serious attention and requires reserving room for migration. It may not correspond to the specific year a quantum attack actually occurs, but it could very well determine whether the industry will still have the resources to cope effectively by then.

data.title: $285 million stolen from Drift: Hackers deliver a fatal blow to DeFi during the bear market.
data.description: Around April 2, 2026, multiple on-chain monitoring and media outlets reported that Drift Protocol, an integrated derivatives and lending protocol within the Solana ecosystem, experienced an abnormal outflow of funds. The project team confirmed that it was under attack, ultimately resulting in the theft of approximately $280 million in funds. The protocol has suspended deposits and withdrawals and is collaborating with security agencies, cross-chain bridges, and trading platforms to address the issue. What is Drift Protocol? Drift is a hybrid DeFi protocol, similar to an exchange: launched in 2021, it started with Solana perpetual contracts and later expanded to spot trading, lending, and more "one-stop-shop" protocol narratives. In 2024, Drift officially stated that the protocol had over $350 million in TVL, more than 175,000 traders, and a cumulative trading volume exceeding $20 billion; in September of the same year, it completed a $25 million Series B, bringing its total funding to $52.5 million. At the mechanism level, Drift documentation explicitly acknowledges its reliance on external oracle accounts and has designed "barriers" including oracle validity, TWAP pruning, price deviation bandwidth verification, and updating market information when necessary to limit specific actions. Its historical narrative has also emphasized that if oracle prices are "invalid or manipulated," it could lead to the depletion of exchange assets in a short period, thus requiring multi-step verification and "multi-range circuit breakers" to create a reaction window. However, this attack demonstrates that even if a protocol has a relatively robust "market risk control barrier," as long as attackers can access or influence the "permission layer" (administrator keys, multi-signature, and risk parameter governance channels), they can potentially turn the barrier itself into a tool that can be misused—for example, by distorting certain thresholds or raising the collateral weight of a certain asset to an irrational level, ultimately allowing the system to "legally" execute asset transfers under the premise that "rules have been rewritten." Drift Protocol Responds to $280 Million Loss: Social Engineering and Durable Nonce Attack Drift Protocol issued a statement regarding today's security incident, stating that a malicious actor gained unauthorized access to the protocol and quickly took over management of the Drift Security Committee using a new attack method involving durable nonces. Drift stated that this was a highly sophisticated attack, seemingly planned and executed in stages over several weeks, including techniques such as pre-signing transactions using durable nonce accounts and delaying execution. According to Drift's current investigation, the incident was not caused by vulnerabilities in Drift programs or smart contracts, and there is no evidence that related mnemonic phrases were leaked. Drift believes the attackers obtained unauthorized or forged transaction approvals before execution, and the durable nonce mechanism and sophisticated social engineering likely played a key role. This incident resulted in the transfer of approximately $280 million in assets from the protocol. Drift stated that the attackers were able to complete the attack through several steps: first, pre-deploying access paths through a durable nonce account; then obtaining sufficient approval authority in multisignature, specifically 2/5 multisignature approval; next, executing a malicious administrator privilege transfer within minutes to gain protocol-level access control; and finally, using this authority to introduce malicious assets and remove all existing withdrawal restrictions, thereby attacking existing funds. Currently, all funds deposited into the lending module, vault, and trading accounts have been affected. Unaffected assets include: DSOL not deposited into Drift, including assets staked to Drift Validators; and insurance fund assets, which will be withdrawn from the protocol and transferred to a more secure environment for protection. As a precaution, Drift has frozen all remaining functionality of the protocol and updated its multisignature configuration, removing affected wallets. This incident has spilled over to multiple DeFi protocols within the Solana ecosystem. Projects such as Reflect Money, Ranger Finance, Neutral Trade, Elemental DeFi, Project 0, Lulo Finance, Asgard Finance, DeFi Carrot, Pyra, xPlace, and Fuse Wallet have been confirmed to be affected, with some projects suspending minting, redemption, or deposit/withdrawal functions. Ranger Finance stated that it faces approximately $900,000 in risk exposure, representing about 6% of its TVL; Pyra indicated that it has suspended related card functionality due to user funds being affected by Drift earnings. Technical Analysis: Fake CVT Tokens and Oracle Manipulation According to previous analysis by Helius developer Ichigo, the Drift Protocol incident may be related to attackers constructing fake CVT tokens and manipulating the Switchboard oracle, thus forming an attack path. He stated that the attackers subsequently used social engineering to enter the security committee governance process and, despite the potential impairment of multi-signature permissions, pushed the token to be listed with high weight as collateral. The attackers minted "fake tokens" weeks in advance and used a pool with extremely low liquidity (approximately $500) on Raydium as a "pricing anchor," then continuously created price and transaction traces through wash trading, paving the way for subsequent oracle price history. It's worth noting that the attackers deposited approximately 20 million CVT tokens with near-zero value, which, after being overvalued to over $100 million, were used as collateral to borrow real assets within the protocol and transfer funds. The total scale is estimated to exceed $200 million. Following the attack on Drift Protocol, its governance token, DRIFT, plummeted by over 40% in the past 24 hours. The annualized negative fee rate for DRIFT U-margined perpetual contracts on major exchanges like Binance has soared to the maximum, exceeding 6,000%, resulting in a massive subsidy from short sellers to long sellers. Timeline of the Incident On March 23, the attackers completed the initial nonce setup. A total of four durable nonce accounts were created that day, two of which were associated with multisignature members of the Drift Security Committee, and the other two were controlled by the attackers. Drift believes this means that at least two-fifths of multisignature signers had signed transactions associated with durable nonce accounts, thus enabling delayed execution. March 25: The suspected attacker's main wallet address (HkGz4KmoZ7Zmk7HN6ndJ31UJ1qZ2qgwQxgVqQwovpZES) obtained initial funds through Near Intents, remained inactive for a long period, and then suddenly activated and received a large amount of funds from the Drift vault on the day of the attack. March 27: Due to a change in security committee members, Drift performed a planned multisignature migration of its security committee. March 30: New durable nonce activity reappeared. A new durable nonce account was created for a member in the updated multisignature. Drift believes this indicates the attacker has once again gained actual usable permissions for 2/5 of the signers in the updated multisignature. On April 1st, the attack entered the execution phase. First, Drift executed a test withdrawal transaction from the insurance fund. Approximately one minute later, the attacker swiftly executed two pre-signed durable nonce transactions, separated by only four slots. The first transaction created and approved a malicious administrator transfer, while the second transaction approved and executed that transfer. At this point, the attacker officially took over key permissions of the protocol. On the afternoon and evening of April 1st, the on-chain monitoring tool MLM detected abnormal fund flows in related addresses, totaling approximately $270.6 million (about 50% of Drift TVL), mainly involving assets such as JLP and USDC. Helius CEO Mert stated that on-chain signs indicated the protocol may have been attacked; Drift subsequently issued a statement confirming that it was monitoring abnormal activity in the protocol and advised users not to deposit funds into the protocol until further notice. Drift stated that the attack was achieved through a combination of two factors: first, a pre-signed durable nonce transaction, allowing the attacker to delay execution at a future point in time; and second, the approval of multiple multi-signature signers was compromised, which was likely achieved through targeted social engineering attacks or transaction information spoofing. Industry Expert Opinions and Governance Reflections Ledger CTO Charles Guillemet stated that the attack was not a smart contract vulnerability, but rather a long-term, latent breach of the multi-signature mechanism. Hackers allegedly gained control of the multi-signature holder's device or private key, misleading operators into approving malicious transactions. This method is highly similar to the Bybit incident last year, which was suspected to be linked to the North Korean hacking group (DPRK). He called on the industry to improve endpoint detection capabilities and adopt hardware-supported plaintext signatures to prevent operational risks. Uniswap founder Hayden Adams bluntly stated that centralized projects must stop calling themselves DeFi; if an administrator's key can drain all funds, it is essentially CeFi. Chaos Labs founder Omer Goldberg added that the Drift protocol's signing keys have complete control over market creation, oracle allocation, and withdrawal limits, and lack time locks, allowing attackers to allegedly steal funds in just about 10 seconds. On-chain tracking shows that the suspected attacker's address (HkGz4KmoZ7Zmk7HN6ndJ31UJ1qZ2qgwQxgVqQwovpZES) quickly transferred/exchanged funds after the attack and then transferred them to Ethereum via Wormhole cross-chain transfer. When some USDC was transferred through the Circle CCTP bridge, Circle failed to freeze the funds in a timely manner, drawing criticism from Delphi Digital co-founder Tommy Shaughnessy and on-chain detective ZachXBT, who argued that Circle was slow to react despite having centralized freezing capabilities. Currently, Drift is collaborating with several security companies to investigate the root cause of the incident and is also working with cross-chain bridges, exchanges, and law enforcement agencies to track and freeze the stolen assets. Drift stated that it will release a more detailed post-incident analysis report in the coming days and welcomes any information or leads related to the investigation.

data.title: Anxiety Economy: Why Transactions Are Exploding
data.description:
Author: Zheng Jie Source: artemis Translation: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Summary

  • In short, an entire generation has calculated the returns of passive investing and found that they are far from sufficient. Financial anxiety will replace greed as the core driving force of trading.

  • The interval between forming market judgments and executing trades is approaching zero.

  • AI will narrow the information gap between retail investors and institutions, but the returns of ordinary active traders will continue to underperform the market.

  • AI will narrow the information gap between retail investors and institutions, but the returns of ordinary active traders will continue to underperform the market.

  • Winners: Robinhood, Coinbase, IBKR Losers: Traditional brokerages selling patience to a speed-driven generation I. The math is clear to everyone Saving $1200 per month, with an 8% annual return, for 24 years. This is the standard answer for someone starting from scratch at age 26 to earn $1 million—by then you'll be 50. You earn $90,000 a year, but after taxes, New York rent, and student loans, you can only actually save $1200 per month. Investing in index funds with an annualized return of 8%: Approximately $220,000 in 10 years; approximately $710,000 in 20 years. Even with a smooth and uneventful investment, one might only reach a million dollars by age 50. This is the baseline scenario assuming everything is done correctly.

    Then you open your phone: your college roommate cashed out $800,000 in startup stock options; your cousin's house bought in 2019 appreciated by 60%; a 24-year-old stranger turned $10,000 of meme coins into $400,000 in just a few weeks. You know these are extreme cases, most people won't post about their losses, but it doesn't matter, you still feel like you're being left far behind.

    So, you start trading.

    ... This is the anxiety economy: an era where financial anxiety becomes the core source of demand for an entire category of products. It serves those who have calculated the costs and benefits and realized that a conservative approach simply cannot achieve their goals. The industry generally believes that the growth in transaction volume stems from: better products, lower fees, more assets, the widespread adoption of AI tools, and improved user experience. These are all true, but not the whole story. The deeper driving force is emotion: the entire industry is serving the same feeling: I'm falling behind, I need a faster way to catch up.

    II. Data Doesn't Lie

    Since 2000:

    • S&P 500 return approximately 400%

    • Home prices have risen approximately 230%

    • Median US wages have only increased by approximately 110%

    IV. Industry Landscape

    The seemingly crowded trading market can actually be clearly divided into four layers:

    1. Discovery Layer: Wallets, deposit channels, data products, and AI tools. Users view prices, receive alerts, deposit funds, and form trading intentions here. Brokerages do not control this layer—you already know what you want to do before you open Robinhood.

    2. Brokerage Layer: Brokerages and Crypto Super Apps.

    3. Brokerage Layer: Brokerages and Crypto Super Apps.

    4. Brokerage Layer: Brokerages and Crypto Super Apps.

      5. Intent translates into action here: click to buy, set up a position, confirm the order. The platform strives to control user funds and transactions; the more funds, the harder it is for users to leave. Trading Venue Layer: Exchanges, perpetual contract platforms, aggregators, prediction markets, tokenized exchanges. Transactions are truly settled here. Globalized assets and 24/7 trading will allow this layer to capture more traffic. Infrastructure Layer: Liquidity, clearing systems, blockchain, tokenized infrastructure. Furthest from users, unconcerned with app wars, only profiting from trading volume. The biggest winners will be: the apps users first open, the platforms that manage funds, and the venues for executing trades. V. Value Flow The ultimate winners will be those companies that enable users to most easily translate this feeling into action.

      Winner

      1. Robinhood is the most obvious winner. From day one, it has served those who "feel behind": zero commission, fractional shares, and a simple interface. Now, it adds prediction markets, gold cards, cryptocurrencies, and retirement accounts, boasting the fastest product iteration speed in fintech. In Q1 2026, it launched the AI ​​assistant Cortex, acquired a CFTC-licensed exchange, and reached 3.9 million gold card users, a year-on-year increase of 77%. Its success lies in understanding: trading volume growth is driven by anxiety. 2. Coinbase's perfect synergy between consumer finance and its 24/7 native marketplace. Controlling crypto deposits and underlying infrastructure, USDC becomes the default savings and settlement tool, and the Base chain achieves profitability. Revenue has shifted from fluctuating transaction fees to subscriptions, stablecoin interest, and infrastructure services. Stablecoin revenue reached $332 million in a single quarter, and USDC's market capitalization reached a record high of $76 billion. Even if users stop trading, their funds remain within the system. 3. Interactive Brokers (IBKR) covers over 170 markets in 40 countries, adding over 1 million new accounts in 2025, bringing the total to 4.4 million accounts and $780 billion in client assets. It supports stablecoin deposits, launched a Visa co-branded card, and redesigned its app to incorporate AI market insights. If a company can maintain its professional depth while achieving Robinhood-like simplicity, it will reap the rewards from high-intent users in the anxiety economy. 4. ICE (NYSE's parent company) collects "tolls." The more tradable instruments, the longer the trading period, and the wider the scope of the market, the more commission it takes. In 2025, it achieved record trading volume, with ADV increasing by 14% year-on-year and revenue reaching $9.9 billion. 5. Hyperliquid: A dark horse in the pure trading venue sector. When traditional markets are closed, it becomes the pricing center for assets such as oil. WTI-related contracts see daily trading volume exceeding $2 billion, a more than 100-fold increase in six months. The 24/7 trading venue offers a structural advantage. Traditional brokerages will be the losers. Companies that don't innovate will ultimately be eliminated. If they continue to sell security and long-term planning to a generation driven by speed and hope, they might retain the assets of baby boomers, but they won't win over the next 100 million traders (Generation Z and Alpha). On watch list: Webull has built a powerful product for active traders—offering realistic charting capabilities, longer trading hours, and a convenient mobile experience. However, its risk lies in its mediocre feature positioning: too complex for beginners, and not deep enough for professionals. eToro—one of the earliest platforms to socialize trading. Copy trading, community dynamics, and the ability to follow the ideas of people who know more than you—in today's anxious economic environment, this intuition that "those who understand the market are more favored" only grows stronger. SoFi remains one of the best examples of integrating financial services (banking, lending, credit, and investment) into a single consumer brand. My concern is its market positioning. When people think of SoFi, do they think of "trading" or "student loan refinancing"? Their product range is extensive, but lacks depth. Every company on the list grows with increasing trading volume. Now you should understand why.

      VI. The “False Advantage” Brought by AI

      AI will reshape trading technologically, but its greater impact lies on the psychological level.

      Imagine this: Before the market opens, an AI briefing summarizes your portfolio, identifies key catalysts, and explains the potential impact of the Consumer Price Index, tariffs, or earnings on your holdings. By the time you've finished your coffee, you already have a clear investment strategy. You feel like you have something in your grasp.

data.title: Layoffs, cryptocurrency sales, AI development: MARA's transformation is just a typical microcosm of mining companies' strategies.
data.description:
Tao Zhu, Jinse Finance

TL;DR

On April 3, 2026, Bitcoin mining company MARA laid off 15% of its workforce to advance its strategic transformation from a pure Bitcoin mining company to an energy and digital infrastructure company, and to increase its investment in AI infrastructure. The company had previously entered the AI ​​computing power market by acquiring a 64% stake in Exaion.
